questions.prompt: A client says 'our website is broken, please fix the DNS.' You check and DNS resolves to the right IP, but the web server returns a 500 error when you load the IP directly. Which layer is the issue at?
choices: Layer 1, registrar.
Layer 2, DNS host.
Layer 3, web host.
Layer 4, mail host.
questions.explanation: The 500 from the web server is at the web host's layer. DNS was never the issue despite the client's framing. Canonical mis-attribution pattern the four-layer model is designed to prevent.

questions.prompt: An audit on a newly-onboarded client domain shows the registrant is 'Previous MSP Pty Ltd' rather than the client. What's the priority fix?
choices: Cosmetic; update it next time you do other work.
Change of registrant is the priority. The process triggers a 60-day outbound-transfer lock at most registrars; do it now while there's no time pressure.
Email the previous MSP and ask them to delete the domain so you can re-register.
Privacy services hide the registrant anyway.
questions.explanation: Until the registrant is changed, the previous MSP is the legal owner. The 60-day post-change lock means do it during calm, not mid-incident.

questions.prompt: WHOIS shows status `redemptionPeriod` and expiry was 38 days ago. What do you do?
choices: Click 'renew' in the registrar panel; the standard renewal will bring it back.
Escalate; redemption recovery requires the registrar's special restore process and a significantly higher fee.
Tell the client the domain is lost and recommend a new one.
Wait until pending delete starts; recovery is easier from there.
questions.explanation: Redemption is past the standard renewal grace; the renew button typically returns an error or quietly fails. Redemption recovery is a different process at the registry and a different fee. Pending delete is past the recovery window.

questions.prompt: A gaining registrar asks the client for the 'EPP code.' The losing registrar's panel shows a field labelled 'Authorization Key.' What's the relationship?
choices: They're different things; you need both.
Same thing. Auth code, EPP code, transfer code, and authorization key are interchangeable terms for the per-domain transfer secret.
EPP code is a newer standard; authorization key is being phased out.
The authorization key is encrypted; the EPP code is the decrypted form.
questions.explanation: The terminology drifts across registrars but the underlying value is one secret. Treat any of these terms as a synonym.

questions.prompt: A client emails: 'register myshop.com.au for us. We're a sole trader without an ABN.' What do you do?
choices: Submit and let auDA reject it after the fact.
Tell the client .com.au requires an ABN or ACN. They can register an ABN via the Australian Business Register first. Surface the gap now.
Refuse to register .com.au for any sole trader on principle.
Suggest a .com instead without mentioning the requirement.
questions.explanation: The eligibility dimension applies; surface the constraint before taking the order so the client decides whether to fix the gap or pick a different TLD. Sole traders with an ABN can register .com.au.

questions.prompt: A vendor's setup instructions say 'point your apex (example.com) at our hostname.' Your DNS host's panel rejects the apex CNAME. What's the workaround?
choices: Apex CNAMEs work everywhere; file a support ticket about the panel bug.
DNS doesn't allow a CNAME at the apex. Use ALIAS / ANAME / CNAME flattening if your DNS host supports it, or redirect the apex to www at the web layer.
Add the CNAME at www and ignore the apex.
Always add an A record at the apex pointing at the vendor's IP, regardless of host capability.
questions.explanation: RFCs 1034 / 2181 prohibit a CNAME at the zone apex. ALIAS / ANAME / flattening resolve the target server-side and publish A/AAAA at the apex, which is standards-compliant.

questions.prompt: A zone has two MX records: `oldmail.example.com.` at priority 10 and `aspmx.l.google.com.` at priority 20. Which does the sending server try first?
choices: aspmx.l.google.com — higher number means main server.
oldmail.example.com — lower priority number is preferred.
Both at once — parallel delivery.
Whichever resolved first in the cache.
questions.explanation: Lower priority = higher preference. Parallel delivery only happens between records at the same priority. The convention 10 / 20 puts the primary first.

questions.prompt: It's fine to have two TXT records on the apex that both start with `v=spf1`, as long as both include the right vendors.
questions.explanation: SPF protocol requires exactly one `v=spf1` TXT per domain. Two SPF records cause receivers to return `permerror` — SPF effectively fails for the domain. Merge into a single TXT instead.

questions.prompt: A planned A-record change is scheduled for Friday at 9pm. Current TTL is 86400. It's Tuesday morning. What do you do today?
choices: Wait until Friday at 9pm and make the change.
Lower the TTL to 300 today, so the lower TTL has time to spread to downstream caches before the cutover.
Lower the TTL on Friday morning.
Lower the TTL on Friday at 8:55pm, five minutes before the change.
questions.explanation: Pre-flight TTL drop with enough lead time is the difference between a 5-minute cutover and a day of inconsistent state. Dropping TTL at the same time as (or just before) the value change doesn't help; downstream caches with the old TTL still hold the old answer.

questions.prompt: A client asks 'how long for DNS propagation?' before a planned A-record change. The current TTL is 300 (you pre-flighted last week). What's the calibrated answer?
choices: 24 to 48 hours, standard for any DNS change.
Most users will see the new value within 5 minutes; some may take a bit longer if their resolver cached just before the change.
Instant for everyone; the authoritative serves the new value immediately.
Up to a week, depending on resolver behaviour.
questions.explanation: The previous TTL is what determines propagation tail. At TTL 300, most caches have re-queried within 5 minutes. The 24-48 hours stock answer is uncalibrated. Saying 'instant' ignores cached state at downstream resolvers.

questions.prompt: A domain-or-DNS ticket lands on an unfamiliar domain. Order the first four checks you run.
items: WHOIS / RDAP on the domain (registrar + delegated nameservers)
dig A example.com against the authoritative nameservers (web host's IP)
dig MX example.com against the authoritative nameservers (mail host)
Match the symptom to the layer responsible
questions.explanation: First identify the four layers (WHOIS for 1 + 2; dig A / MX against authoritative for 3 + 4), then match symptom to layer. Acting on the wrong layer wastes time.

questions.prompt: A tech finishes the three courses and passes the final scenario assessment with 85%. What does the result actually authorise?
choices: The tech is signed off to work all client domains unsupervised.
The tech is ready for the MSP's own shadow-and-sign-off process.
The tech now has authority to override the ceiling on items they feel confident about.
The tech can execute DNSSEC operations without sign-off if they're confident.
questions.explanation: The LMS pass is a recommendation of readiness, not the MSP's sign-off. The two layers are different: LMS says the tech can make the calls; MSP review says the tech makes them well on real customers.
